]]><![CDATA[Kings Weekly Podcast]]>https://www.basketballintelligence.net/p/kings-weekly-podcast-78chttps://www.basketballintelligence.net/p/kings-weekly-podcast-78cTue, 07 Apr 2026 14:19:10 GMTNick and Ray finish their three-part series on the Sacramento Kings rookie class with Dylan Cardwell, the undrafted free agent sensation who signed a two-way contract with the Kings after the 2025 NBA Draft. They cover his exceptional defensive game, as well as some areas for improvement. They talk about his shot-blocking prowess, his relentlessness on the glass, and his issues with fouling. They also discuss his efficient offense around the basket and potential areas for improvement on that end of the floor. Finally, they wrap up by picking song lyrics that describe their thoughts on the current state of the Sacramento Kings

]]><![CDATA[Kings Weekly Podcast]]>https://www.basketballintelligence.net/p/kings-weekly-podcast-6eahttps://www.basketballintelligence.net/p/kings-weekly-podcast-6eaSun, 05 Apr 2026 15:02:27 GMTNick and Ray continue their three-part series on the Kings rookie class with Maxime Raynaud, the 42nd pick in the 2025 NBA Draft. They cover his remarkably versatile offensive game, as well as some areas for improvement. They talk about his incredible fluidity and handle for his size, his efficiency around the basket, and his burgeoning shooting potential from beyond the arc. They also discuss his defense, including his excellent communication, but also his struggles with defending around the rim. They wrap up by picking a song title that describes their thoughts on the chances that the Kings have (from an optimistic viewpoint) of contending by the 2029-2030 season.

“Yeah, for sure,” Kalkbrenner said. “We’re a young team. You don’t have it all figured out right away. Taking every opportunity we can to grow, have those character wins, is big for us. Every time you take a step further, it’s good. Just got to keep building.”

That’s essentially the thesis of this Hornets season in a handful of words. They’re not a finished product, and that should be obvious. But they’re also not waiting to become one before they take the moment seriously. They’re building in real time, in real games, with real consequences.

The second concept goes even further, a 22-team lottery that brings in the four teams eliminated in the first round of the playoffs. It also uses a two-year record component and a minimum win threshold concept, like 25 wins, to discourage extreme tanks.

This is the one that makes the biggest chunk of people uneasy because it introduces the most subjectivity and the most edge cases. A two-year formula can punish teams that crater for reasons that have nothing to do with intentional losing, and a win floor can feel like the league retroactively assigning results to smooth out the standings

His greatest contributions have come as a connector, ranking in the 88th percentile by creating 1.04 points per possession as a pick-and-roll ball handler despite shooting 40.4% from the field himself. His isolation efficiency dropped to the 6th percentile of the league with 0.57 PPP. Tatum mentioned not feeling fully confident in his burst until the loss to the Spurs, where he drove past Victor Wembanyama after a jab-step. He’s received fewer opportunities to create, ranking fourth on the team with 65.8 touches per game (2.4 seconds on average), compared to his team-leading 84.9 (3.7 sec. avg.) last year.

BOS: 10 takeaways from a lackadaisical Celtics loss
Azad Rosay, Celtics Blog
